metadata.title: International Trade
metadata.subtitle: Understanding Global Markets and Your Everyday Life

content.title: International Trade
content.subtitle: Understanding Global Markets and Your Everyday Life

content.label: Look at What You're Wearing Right Now
content.question:
Check the tags. Where were your clothes made? Probably Vietnam, Bangladesh, or China. Your shoes? China, Vietnam, Indonesia. Your phone? Assembled in China with parts from 43 countries.

Your iPhone: Designed in California | Parts from 43 countries | Assembled in China | Software from USA | Sold worldwide

This is GLOBALIZATION—your everyday life depends on international trade!

objectives.description: comparative advantage and why countries trade
benefits and costs of international trade for consumers, businesses, and workers
how tariffs, quotas, and trade policies affect prices and jobs
how exchange rates affect travel costs and import/export prices

terms.definition: Countries benefit by specializing in what they do relatively best—even if one country is better at everything
terms.example: USA focuses on high-tech and finance, China on manufacturing—both benefit from trade

terms.definition: Tax on imported goods that raises prices and protects domestic producers
terms.example: 25% tariff on imported cars makes foreign cars $7,500 more expensive

terms.definition: Limit on the quantity of imports allowed into a country
terms.example: Restricting the number of foreign cars that can enter the country annually

terms.definition: Price of one currency in terms of another—determines how much foreign currency you get for your dollar
terms.example: $1 USD = 150 Japanese Yen, 0.92 Euro, or 7.3 Chinese Yuan

terms.definition: When a country imports more than it exports—buying more from other countries than selling to them
terms.example: US imports more goods from China than it exports to China

terms.definition: Government policies to protect domestic industries from foreign competition through tariffs, quotas, and regulations
terms.example: Using tariffs and quotas to shield local manufacturers from cheaper imports

leftPanel.title: Simple Example: Roommates
paragraphs: You can clean apartment in 1 hour, roommate takes 3 hours.
You can cook dinner in 2 hours, roommate takes 4 hours.
You're better at BOTH! Should you do everything?
NO!
Optimal Arrangement: You cook (lower opportunity cost), Roommate cleans (lower relative cost).
Result: Both tasks done in less total time!

highlightBox.text: Specializing in your comparative advantage creates gains for both parties—even if one is absolutely better at everything!

infoCard.title: Same applies to countries!
items: USA specializes in sectors requiring advanced technology and capital
Consumers in both countries get more goods at lower prices

leftColumn.title: For Consumers (YOU)
items: Lower Prices: iPhone $800 vs. $2,000+ if made entirely in USA
More Variety: Bananas year-round, electronics from Asia, European cars
Greater Choice: Access to products not made domestically

rightColumn.title: For Businesses & Economy
items: Larger Markets: Sell to billions of customers globally
Cheaper Inputs: Import materials at lower cost
Export Jobs: Often higher-paying than domestic-only jobs
Tech Transfer: Learn from other countries' innovations

leftPanel.title: Job Displacement
paragraphs: The Hard Truth: Trade creates winners AND losers
1990: 17.7 million US manufacturing jobs
2024: 12.8 million manufacturing jobs
Lost: ~5 million jobs
Who's hurt: Factory workers, manufacturing communities, older workers hard to retrain
These job losses are concentrated in specific industries and regions, causing severe economic disruption for affected communities.

stats.label: College-educated benefit (export sector jobs), Less-educated hurt (manufacturing jobs lost)

infoCard.title: National Security Concerns
items: Dependence on other countries for critical goods:
• Rare earth minerals (China) - essential for tech

leftPanel.title: How Tariffs Work
paragraphs: Tariff = Tax on imported goods
Example: 25% Tariff on Cars
Foreign car import cost: $30,000
Final price: $37,500
This makes foreign cars more expensive, protecting domestic car manufacturers from competition.

highlightBox.text: Recent Example: Trump Tariffs (2018-19) imposed 25% tariffs on $250B of Chinese goods to protect US manufacturing. China retaliated with tariffs on US agriculture, hurting farmers.

stats.label: Protects domestic jobs - Foreign goods more expensive → domestic more competitive
Government revenue - Tariff payments can fund programs
Hurts consumers - Higher prices, less choice, reduced purchasing power
Retaliation risk - Trade wars hurt everyone, reducing exports

leftPanel.title: Understanding Exchange Rates
paragraphs: Exchange Rate = Price of one currency in terms of another
Current Examples:
$1 USD = 150 Japanese Yen
$1 USD = 0.92 Euro
$1 USD = 7.3 Chinese Yuan

highlightBox.text: Travel Example: Hotel costs €100/night. At $1 = €0.92: €100/0.92 = $108.70. At $1 = €1.00: €100/1.00 = $100.00. Strong dollar = Cheaper travel abroad!

stats.label: Cheaper to travel abroad - Your dollars buy more foreign currency
Cheaper imports - Foreign goods cost less in dollars
Hurts US exporters - US goods become expensive abroad

layoutData.title: Three Key Factors Drive Exchange Rate Changes
bulletPoints: Interest Rates: Higher US interest rates → Foreign investors buy dollars to earn higher returns → Dollar strengthens (Fed policy from L-53 directly affects exchange rates!)
Economic Growth: Strong US economic growth → Investors want dollars to invest in growing economy → Dollar strengthens
Inflation: Higher US inflation → Dollar loses purchasing power → Dollar weakens (L-54 connection: Inflation erodes currency value)
layoutData.keyPoint: Exchange rates connect to everything you've learned: Fed policy (L-53), inflation (L-54), and economic growth all influence the value of the dollar

content.question:
The Federal Reserve just raised interest rates to fight inflation. This strengthens the dollar.

Winners: Consumers buying imports, travelers going abroad
Losers: US exporters, American farmers selling overseas

Should the Fed consider exchange rate effects when setting interest rates? Who should government prioritize?

content.headerTitle: Real-World Example: Free Trade vs. Protectionism

leftColumn.title: Free Trade Position
items: 2. More variety and quality through competition
3. Economic efficiency (produce what you're best at)
4. Global economic growth creates more prosperity
5. Peace through interdependence (trading partners don't fight)
"Free trade is a win-win. Both countries benefit from specialization."

rightColumn.title: Protectionist Position
items: "We need to protect American jobs and workers."

scenario.name: Should Apple Make iPhones in the USA?
paragraphs: Current Situation: iPhones are designed in California but assembled in China using parts from 43 countries. This keeps costs low and allows Apple to sell iPhones for ~$800.
Protectionist Proposal: Require Apple to manufacture iPhones entirely in the USA to create American jobs.
Analysis: Manufacturing in the USA would require paying workers $25-40/hour instead of $3-6/hour, plus building new facilities. Economists estimate this would increase iPhone cost to $2,000-3,000.
The Trade-off: Create ~100,000 US manufacturing jobs BUT consumers pay $1,200-2,200 more per phone. With 50 million iPhones sold annually in US, that's $60-110 billion in extra consumer costs.

outcomes.label: Current: Made in China
outcomes.detail: Affordable for most Americans, but manufacturing jobs overseas

outcomes.label: Made in USA
outcomes.detail: US jobs created, but phone becomes luxury item, fewer people can afford

content.question:
Will YOUR future career be affected by globalization?

Threatened by outsourcing: Manufacturing, customer service, some tech jobs
Opportunities in export sectors: Engineering, finance, agriculture, entertainment

How can you prepare for a global economy? What skills are harder to outsource?

leftColumn.title: What Most Economists Agree On
items: Total benefits of trade > total costs
All consumers benefit from lower prices
Can compensate losers with retraining and assistance
Trade restrictions hurt consumers more than they help workers

rightColumn.title: But They Acknowledge
items: Job losses are REAL and PAINFUL for affected workers
Benefits are diffuse (small savings per person)
Need better policies to help displaced workers
Winners rarely compensate losers in practice

questions.question: Explain comparative advantage using the roommates example. Why should you specialize even if you're better at both tasks?
How do tariffs protect domestic jobs but hurt consumers? Use the car tariff example to explain.
Explain how a strong dollar helps consumers but hurts exporters. Why can't both win?
Why do 90% of economists support free trade, yet many workers support protectionism? Who's right?

takeaways.title: Comparative advantage drives trade
takeaways.description: Countries benefit by specializing in what they do relatively best, even if one is better at everything

takeaways.title: Trade creates winners and losers
takeaways.description: Consumers benefit from lower prices; some workers lose jobs to foreign competition

takeaways.title: Tariffs protect jobs but raise prices
takeaways.description: Trade restrictions help specific industries but hurt all consumers through higher prices

takeaways.title: Exchange rates affect purchasing power
takeaways.description: Strong dollar makes travel and imports cheaper but hurts US exporters

takeaways.title: No easy answers in trade policy
takeaways.description: Free trade vs. protectionism involves real trade-offs between efficiency and equity

main.title: Tomorrow's Learning Lab
main.description: Use the Currency Exchange Simulator and Trade Impact Analyzer to see how global trade affects YOUR life and career

steps: Analyze your state's role in the global economy (exports, imports, trade-dependent jobs)
Trace supply chains for products you buy daily
Use Currency Exchange Simulator to plan international travel
Simulate tariff effects on consumer prices and jobs
Develop your personal career strategy for the global economy

content.tagline: "In a globalized economy, understanding trade helps you make smarter consumer and career decisions"
